plainMeaning: Even today Mahāvīra's pure path still flows on unbroken in our world, so may that Lord Vardhamāna, the sun of the three worlds, be ever victorious.
recitation: ajja vi vahaï sutitthaṁ, akhaṇḍiaṁ jassa bharahavāsaṁmi | so vaddhamāṇasāmī, tiyalukka divāyaro jayaü ||

wordByWord.etymology: From ajja (Skt. adya, 'today') + vi (Skt. api, the emphatic 'even'); the two are written joined here.
wordByWord.notes: Stresses present, living continuity — the hinge-word of the verse. Often printed as two words 'ajja vi'.

wordByWord.etymology: 3rd sg. present of Prakrit √vah (Skt. √vah, vahati) 'to flow, carry, bear'.
wordByWord.notes: Chosen for the tīrtha ('ford') image: the teaching keeps 'flowing' like an unbroken stream.

wordByWord.gloss: the good (holy) ford — the pure tīrtha/teaching
wordByWord.etymology: su- ('good, excellent') + titthaṁ (Skt. tīrtha, 'ford'); accusative singular, object of vahaï.
wordByWord.notes: Object of vahaï; tīrtha already glossed in the preface.

wordByWord.etymology: Privative a- + khaṇḍia (Skt. khaṇḍita, 'broken, split'); accusative singular agreeing with sutitthaṁ.
wordByWord.notes: Emphatic: the ford has run on with no break since Mahāvīra's time.

wordByWord.etymology: Genitive singular of the relative pronoun ja- (Skt. yad, gen. yasya).
wordByWord.notes: Relative whose correlative is 'so' (he) in the next line — 'whose ford flows… he is Vardhamāna'.

wordByWord.etymology: bharaha (Skt. Bharata) + vāsa (Skt. varṣa/vāsa, 'region, continent, abode') + locative ending -ṁmi (Skt. -smin).
wordByWord.notes: Locative of place. Bharata-kṣetra already glossed in the preface.

wordByWord.etymology: Nominative singular masculine of the demonstrative ta- (Skt. saḥ).
wordByWord.notes: Correlative resolving the relative jassa; points emphatically to Vardhamāna.

wordByWord.etymology: vaddhamāṇa (Skt. Vardhamāna, 'the ever-increasing') + sāmī (Skt. svāmī, 'lord, master').
wordByWord.notes: Vardhamāna and Mahāvīra already glossed in the preface; sāmī ('lord') is the only added element.

wordByWord.etymology: ti (Skt. tri, 'three') + loka/luka (Skt. loka, 'world'); = triloka, the lower, middle and upper realms.
wordByWord.notes: Qualifies divāyaro. Variant readings tialoya / tiloya circulate; the printed form is tiyalukka (see notes).

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit for Skt. divākara: divā ('by day') + kara ('maker') — 'maker of day', the sun.
wordByWord.notes: Predicative epithet of Vardhamāna: 'the sun of the three worlds'.

wordByWord.gloss: may he be victorious! / all hail!
wordByWord.etymology: Benedictive/imperative 3rd sg. of Prakrit √jaya (Skt. √ji, jayatu) 'to conquer, prevail'.
wordByWord.notes: The verse-closing acclamation, echoing 'jayai' that opened v.1.

literalTranslation: Even today the good ford flows on unbroken — he in whose land of Bharata (it flows) — that Lord Vardhamāna, the sun of the three worlds, may he be victorious!
idiomaticTranslation: Even now his holy ford runs on, unbroken, here in the land of Bharata; so may that Lord Vardhamāna, the very sun of all three worlds, be ever victorious!

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Where v.1 painted Vīra's lotus-eyes and elephant-stride, v.2 lifts the gaze from his body to his ongoing work in time and space, and from acclaim to benediction. Two claims are pressed. First, the temporal one: 'ajja-vi' — even today, long after the Jina walked, his 'su-tittha', the good ford, is still 'vahaï', still flowing. The ford-and-stream conceit is deliberate: a tīrtha is precisely a crossing-place over water, and the teaching that ferries beings across saṁsāra is imagined as a living current that has not run dry. The reinforcing 'akhaṇḍiaṁ' (unbroken) insists there has been no rupture in the line of transmission. Second, the spatial-doctrinal anchoring: this stream flows 'bharahavāsaṁmi', in Bharata-kṣetra — our very region, where Tīrthaṅkaras arise and their order endures. The relative 'jassa' (whose) is then snapped shut by the correlative 'so' (he): the one whose ford still flows is none other than 'Vaddhamāṇasāmī'. The verse crowns him with a fresh image not in the v.1 simile-set — 'tiyalukka divāyaro', the sun of the three worlds. Against the cooler lunar-and-lotus register of the hymn, the solar metaphor is pointed: the sun is the dispeller of darkness and the source by which all else is seen, so Vīra illumines the lower, middle and upper realms alike. The closing 'jayaü' (may he be victorious) answers the opening 'jayai' of v.1, framing these two gāthās — the poet's announced 'gāhā-juala' — as a single bracket of victory-cry around the portrait.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse-specific point is the doctrine of an enduring tīrtha (śāsana). 'Tīrtha' here is not a pilgrimage spot but the fourfold order and the path it carries; calling it 'akhaṇḍa' affirms that Mahāvīra's dispensation persists through this declining (avasarpiṇī) age until its appointed end — the ground on which a present-day reciter can still take refuge. Locating it 'in Bharata' invokes Jain cosmography: it is precisely in the karma-bhūmi of Bharata-kṣetra that a Tīrthaṅkara establishes a ford. The 'divākara' epithet carries the standard Jain valence of light as knowledge: as the physical sun ends night, the Jina's kevala-jñāna ends the darkness of moha — and 'of the three worlds' universalizes that illumination beyond Bharata to the whole loka.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Reciting this gāthā, the practitioner affirms that the path is not a museum-piece but a current still flowing 'today' — the same teaching one is at that moment practising in pratikramaṇa. The benedictive 'jayaü' turns remembrance into active homage: one does not merely recall Vīra but wills his victory and, by that, aligns with the light one asks to prevail.

crossReferences.text: Logassa (Caturviṁśati-stava) — Mahāvīra as the last of the 24, praised with light-imagery ('āiccesu adhiyaṁ payāsayarā')
crossReferences.source: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha ed.)

crossReferences.text: Nandī-sūtra Sajjhāy — the saṅgha/tīrtha imaged as an enduring city, chariot and wheel
crossReferences.source: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha ed.)

sources.title: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipaksha edition)
sources.consultedFor: Verbatim Gujarati-script Prakrit text of Vīrastava gāthā 2 and its placement as the 3rd of the Nine Smaraṇas.

sources.title: Jainqq.org — Nava Smaraṇa / Jaina stotra collections
sources.consultedFor: Standard nava-smaraṇa ordering and the textual identity of the Vīrastava gāthās.

sources.title: Pandit Hargovinddas T. Sheth, Pāia-sadda-mahaṇṇavo (Prakrit–Sanskrit dictionary)
sources.consultedFor: Prakrit glosses for vahaï (√vah, to flow/bear), tittha (tīrtha), khaṇḍia, and the benedictive jayaü.

sources.title: Monier-Williams Sanskrit–English Dictionary (Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon)
sources.consultedFor: Sanskrit etyma divākara ('day-maker', sun), tīrtha ('ford'), triloka ('three worlds'), and Vardhamāna.

notes: The core sense is secure and matches the preface (the tīrtha still flows unbroken in Bharata; Vardhamāna hailed as the sun of the three worlds). Variant readings: (a) 'tiyalukka' appears in other editions as tialoya/tiloya/tihuaṇa — all = triloka; the printed conjunct is honoured here. (b) 'ajjavi' is sometimes set as two words 'ajja vi'. (c) 'sutitthaṁ' could be read either as 'su-tittha' (the excellent ford) or simply 'the holy tīrtha'; rendered as 'good/holy ford' to keep the stream-image that 'vahaï' demands. The solar image 'divāyaro' is specific to this gāthā and is distinct from the moon of the hymn's recurring lotus–elephant–moon triad, so it is treated as a fresh epithet, not part of that triad. Per the book's orthography policy the Gujarati-script Prakrit mātrās/anusvāras as printed are authoritative; IAST transliterations here are approximate.
